retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Granulation Tissue -- Cellular Level -- Fibroblasts
retrieved_docs.content: This cell type forms the granulation tissue extracellular matrix (ECM). The ECM initially consists of type III collagen, a rapidly produced and weaker form of collagen that is ultimately replaced by the stronger type I collagen at the end of wound healing and scar formation. Transforming growth factor (TGF)-β is a growth factor produced by fibroblasts and keratinocytes during wound healing. It has been shown to induce granulation tissue formation and the differentiation of myofibroblasts.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Fibroblast -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Fibroblast -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Fibroblasts can regenerate functional tissue. They have involvement in all three stages of wound healing: inflammation, cell proliferation, ECM deposition, and remodeling. The process starts with an injury triggering an inflammatory response. Clot formation begins at the wound site formed from a fibrin-fibronectin matrix. Fibroblasts work off this scaffold to initiate the deposition and remodeling process. [6] Fibroblasts travel to the site of trauma after chemotactic stimulation. Fibroblast migration is a dynamic process involving ECM synthesis in the proximal direction and release of previously created distal structures. Collagenases produced by the fibroblast destroy neighboring interstitial matrix and assist in the migratory process. During migration, tractional forces are created in a parallel manner along anticipated lines of stress. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Granulation Tissue -- Cellular Level -- Myofibroblasts
retrieved_docs.content: Myofibroblasts are responsible for the contractile process in wound closure. When granulation tissue forms, fibroblasts slowly modulate into myofibroblasts, characterized by bundles of actin microfilaments along their cell's plasma membrane. Prostaglandins, bradykinins, epinephrine, and norepinephrine modulate the contraction function by the myofibroblasts. Eventually, the actin binds to the extracellular component fibronectin, attaches to the collagen fibers, retracts, and draws collagen fibers toward it. The mass effect causes wound contraction and closure.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Fibroblast -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Fibroblasts proliferate at the site of injury mediated by a slew of cytokines and growth factors. It is during this proliferative stage that granulation tissue forms and fibroblast activation takes place. The TGF-B above is one of the most well-researched cytokine mediators of fibroblast function. It functions as a chemotactic agent, a trigger for both proliferation and apoptosis, an enzyme activator, and a regulator of collagen deposition. [5] Present in low concentrations, TGF-B activates fibroblast cell proliferation; Conversely, it inhibits fibroblast mitogenesis when present in high quantities. TGF-B can come from platelets, white blood cells, parenchymal and epithelial cells, or from the fibroblast itself. [12] The fibroblast is necessary to synthesize the ECM scaffold to replace damaged tissue. The ECM is composed primarily of collagen, proteoglycans, and glycoproteins. In homeostasis, the primary collagen type produced by the fibroblast is organ dependent. Type I is predominant in the skin, I and III are prevalent in the interstitium of the lung, liver, and kidney, and IV collagen is abundant in the alveolar basement membrane. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Granulation Tissue -- Cellular Level -- Endothelial Cells
retrieved_docs.content: The endothelial cells are responsible for revascularization and angiogenesis at the site of the wound. Initially quiescent, the resident endothelial cells become activated by several angiogenic factors, including fibroblast growth factor, vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF), platelet-derived growth factor (PDGF), angiogenin, and TGF-α and TGF-β. Once activated, the endothelial cells undergo 4 events in the formation of new blood vessels: Protease production (breakdown of ECM) Chemotaxis Proliferation Remodeling and differentiation

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Granulation Tissue -- Cellular Level -- Keratinocytes
retrieved_docs.content: In an autocrine and paracrine fashion, keratinocytes produce signals that regulate more keratinocyte activation and stimulate other cell types (fibroblasts) during wound closure. Interleukin (IL)-1 increases keratinocyte migration and proliferation, activates nearby fibroblasts, and increases KGF. TNF-α acts similarly to IL-1 and activates the FGF (fibroblast growth factor) pathway. TGF-β, produced by keratinocytes and fibroblasts, reverts keratinocytes to their basal cell phenotype. This signaling process is deficient in venous ulcers and contributes to nonhealing (see Image. Chronic Lipodermatosclerosis and Venous Stasis Ulceration).

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Granulation Tissue -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Granulation tissue is considered a contractile organ, characterized histologically by the presence and proliferation of fibroblasts, keratinocytes, endothelial cells, new thin-walled capillaries, and inflammatory cell infiltration of the extracellular matrix. Granulation tissue formation is complex and requires an intricate interplay between the cell types at the wound site (see Image. Granulation Tissue, H/E, 20x).

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Fibroblast -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Dupuytren contracture is a hand deformity that slowly forces fingers into a bent position. Due to an unknown etiology, proliferation of fibroblasts arranged in cords, nodules of myofibroblasts, and disordered collagen deposition causes disfiguration of the hand. Fibroblasts propagate this condition generating profibrotic cytokines in a prolonged manner subsequently leading to myofibroblast activation and continuous cytokine production. The pathophysiology of connective tissue contracture lies not only within the tension produced by myofibroblasts by also by the dynamic shortening of the ECM remodeling itself. [11] [5] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Osteoprogenitor Cells -- Tissue Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: The collected nucleated cells are plated at 100,000 cells/cm^2 density in supplemented CM. The CM supplement requires an additional 5 ng/mL fibroblast growth factor-2 (GF2) and 10 nmol/L dexamethasones; both elements proliferate osteogenic commitment of bone marrow stromal cells. The supplemental process undergoes humidification at 37 degrees C in a 5% CO2 incubator. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Integument -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: The proliferative phase involves the multiplication of resident cells such as keratinocytes and fibroblasts that contribute to the formation of granulation tissue. Through a matrix of immune cells and the eventual formation of a collagen network by fibroblasts and myofibroblasts, the new extracellular matrix forms. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Fibroblast -- Structure
retrieved_docs.content: Fibroblasts stem from a mesenchymal origin and have an elongated spindle or stellate shape with a multitude of cytoplasmic projections. Within the cytoplasm is an abundance of rough endoplasmic reticulum (rER) and a large Golgi apparatus. Myofibroblasts appear similarly to both fibroblasts and smooth muscle cells with additional distinguished features of nuclear membrane folds and long collections of microfilaments connecting to surrounding myofibroblasts and the extracellular matrix. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Bone Age -- Introduction -- Techniques:
retrieved_docs.content: Chondrocyte physiology: The thyroid hormone regulates the growth and maturation of bones by regulating cartilage proliferation, differentiation, and osteogenesis. It facilitates growth hormone action. Cartilage is made up of cells (chondroblasts and chondrocytes) and extracellular matrix (10% aggrecan, 75% water, collagen, and other constituents). Chondroblasts initially secrete the extracellular matrix, inside which they become trapped and mature into chondrocytes. The outermost layer of cartilage is a layer of dense irregular connective tissue called perichondrium, which is constituted by an inner layer of chondroblasts and an outer layer of fibroblasts. In growing cartilage, chondrocytes divide and develop into nests of 2 to 4 cells. These cells are situated in matrix-enclosed compartments called lacunae. The size of its lacuna is a good criterion in deciding whether a chondrocyte is young or old. The larger the lacuna, the older the chondrocyte and vice versa. Active, young chondrocytes are secretory and contain basophilic cytoplasm with the rich, rough endoplasmic reticulum (RER). Older chondrocytes contain fat droplets.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Granulation Tissue -- Mechanism -- Proliferative Stage
retrieved_docs.content: During this stage of healing, re-epithelialization and the replacement of the clot by granulation tissue occurs simultaneously. This stage is highly cellular, with the primary cell types including fibroblasts, keratinocytes, and endothelial cells. Fibroblasts function to synthesize collagen and the extracellular matrix. The granulation tissue comprises an extracellular matrix, proteoglycans, hyaluronic acid, collagen, and elastin. Cytokines, growth factors, interleukins, and angiogenesis factors are active during this time and can continue anywhere from days to weeks under normal circumstances. Granulation tissue grows from the base of the wound and can typically fill any size of the wound. Any errors in granulation tissue formation can result in chronic wound formation. Foreign bodies at the site of a wound can also result in persistent granulation tissue and poor wound healing, with excess macrophages, fibroblasts, and capillaries reacting around the foreign material.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Fibroblast -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Fibroblasts are the most common cell type represented in connective tissue. These cells produce a diverse group of products including collagen type I, III, and IV, proteoglycans, fibronectin, laminins, glycosaminoglycans, metalloproteinases, and even prostaglandins. In the adult body, fibroblasts remain in a quiescent form until stimuli activate protein synthesis and contractile mechanisms. These cells synthesize reorganize the ECM found in the skin, lung, heart, kidney, liver, eye, and other organs. The ECM is in constant communication with the surrounding cells as fibroblasts can secrete and respond to both autocrine and paracrine signals. [3] [4] Matrix reorganization occurs through a process of degradation and crosslinking enzymes, produced by fibroblasts, that are activated and regulated by pro-inflammatory cytokines and growth factors. Transcription growth factor-alpha and beta (TGF-A and TGF-B), platelet-derived growth factor (PDGF), granulocyte-macrophage colony-stimulating factor (GM-CSF), epidermal growth factor (EGF), and tumor necrosis factor (TNF) all have implications in fibroblast regulation. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Fibroblast -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Fibroblasts are a diverse group of cells.  Within one organ system, there can be a great variety of functions. Within the integument, dermal fibroblasts in different locations have separate roles. The superficially located lineage involves the formation of the hair follicle and is responsible for reepithelization during wound healing; the deeper lineage is responsible for ECM generation. [4] Fibroblasts are known for their plasticity; adipocytes, pericytes, endothelial and epithelial cells, otherwise known as terminally differentiated cells, can de-differentiate into fibroblasts. [6] Stimulation of fibroblasts further increases susceptibility to epigenetic modifications. The ability of fibroblasts to transform is partly due to the variety of cell-surface adhesion receptors (integrins, syndecans, cadherins) that facilitate the communication of fibroblasts with their surroundings. One of these well-described fibroblast transformations is the transformation of fibroblast into the myofibroblast. [7] Myofibroblasts are present in both healthy and pathologic tissues and contain features of fibroblasts and smooth muscle cells. [3] These cells work in conjunction with vascular endothelial cells to form granulation tissue during times of wound healing.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 997
retrieved_docs.content: Intervillous hemorrhage of placenta is a disorder characterized by bleeding into the placental intervillous space and is regarded as a condition affecting the fetal membranes or amniotic fluid; it occurs in the context of a pregnancy in which a child is present.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2634
retrieved_docs.content: (تنكس حصوي) and choroidal atrophy (تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة), while localized calcification around the optic disc (حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي) represents a specific form of mineral deposition. Finally, choroidal scarring (ندبة مشيمية شبكية) denotes fibrotic remodeling after injury or inflammation. Collectively, these entities underscore the choroidea’s central role in ocular disease, highlighting the need for careful clinical assessment and targeted management of choroidal involvement across diverse pathological contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 266
retrieved_docs.content: and retina, leading to scarring, atrophy, detachment, neovascularization and visual impairment. Accurate identification of the specific choroidal and retinal pathology is essential for comprehensive evaluation, multidisciplinary management and optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 2062
retrieved_docs.content: CAESAREAN SECTION SCAR TYPE UNKNOWN is clinically associated with both الانفصال المبكر للمشيمة (early placental separation) and مخاض قبل الأوان (preterm labor). In addition, REPRODUCTIVE HISTORY POOR is clinically associated with مخاض قبل الأوان, indicating that an unknown‑type cesarean section scar and a poor reproductive history are both risk factors for preterm labor, while the unknown‑type scar also predisposes to early placental separation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1238
retrieved_docs.content: malformation of the choroid (الأَتْلامُ وِعائِيَّةُ الشَّكل بالمشيمية), partial perimacular choroidal calcification (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي), and hereditary choroidal calcification (حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي). Collectively, these entities illustrate that the choroid is a central site for diverse pathological processes, and recognizing the specific type of choroidal involvement—whether inflammatory, scar‑forming, degenerative, traumatic, metastatic, or congenital—is essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted therapy, and optimal patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2317
retrieved_docs.content: CAESAREAN SECTION SCAR TYPE UNKNOWN is clinically associated with الانفصال المبكر للمشيمة, and الانفصال المبكر للمشيمة is clinically associated with بضع الرحم. This indicates that an unidentified type of caesarean section scar may increase the risk of early placental separation, which in turn is linked to uterine atony, underscoring the need for vigilant obstetric assessment in affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1697
retrieved_docs.content: A caesarean section scar of unknown type and a poor reproductive history are clinically associated with مخاض قبل الأوان (preterm labor), indicating that women who have an indeterminate type of previous cesarean scar or who have experienced suboptimal reproductive outcomes are at increased risk for premature labor. Recognizing these associations enables clinicians to identify high‑risk patients, implement closer surveillance, and consider preventive strategies to reduce the likelihood of preterm delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 129
retrieved_docs.content: such as حمل مَبيضِيّ (white‑placenta pregnancy), مشيمة ملتصقة (adherent placenta), and ورق خبيث في المشيمة (malignant chorionic villi) are also grouped within SMQ, illustrating additional high‑risk conditions that require comprehensive risk evaluation and integrated monitoring and therapeutic strategies to ensure maternal and fetal safety.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 161

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1238
retrieved_docs.content: choroid scar all represent reparative or fibrotic sequelae that localize to the choroidal layer after injury or inflammation. Degenerative changes are represented by partial generalized choroidal dystrophy, senile choroidal atrophy (الضمور الشيخوخي بالمشيمية), and secondary spread of choroidal atrophy (انتشار الضمور الثانوي بالمشيمية), reflecting age‑related or progressive loss of choroidal structure and function. Traumatic disruptions of the choroid include choroidal rupture (التمزق المشيمي) and choroidal inversion (تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة), while fluid‑related detachments are described as serous choroidal detachment (انفصال المشيمية المصلية) and general choroidal detachment of the eye (انفصال مشيمية العين). Congenital and hereditary abnormalities are captured by the terms congenital malformation of the choroid (الأَتْلامُ وِعائِيَّةُ الشَّكل بالمشيمية), partial perimacular choroidal calcification (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي), and hereditary choroidal calcification (حَثَلُ

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 1160
retrieved_docs.content: hereditary choroidal calcification (حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي) reflect abnormal calcium accumulation within the choroidal stroma. Collectively, these entities illustrate that the choroidea is a common locus for diverse pathological processes, and recognizing the specific type of choroidal involvement—whether hemorrhagic, inflammatory, traumatic, dystrophic, detachment‑related, or depositional—is essential for accurate diagnosis, appropriate management, and optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1845
retrieved_docs.content: CAESAREAN SECTION SCAR TYPE UNKNOWN is clinically associated with cesarean section, indicating that patients who have undergone a cesarean delivery may present with a scar of unspecified type, a factor that clinicians should consider when evaluating postoperative healing, potential complications, and future obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 141
retrieved_docs.content: lesions, fetal demise, and undefined obstetric indications within a comprehensive framework that guides clinicians in assessing how each factor may affect maternal and fetal outcomes and informs comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 5 -- Community 2925
retrieved_docs.content: The condition described as “CAESAREAN SECTION SCAR TYPE UNKNOWN” is clinically associated with “ولادة قبل الأوان” (preterm birth). This relationship suggests that when a caesarean section scar cannot be classified, there may be an increased likelihood of delivering before term, highlighting the importance of careful obstetric monitoring and management in patients with indeterminate scar types.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1702
retrieved_docs.content: CAESAREAN SECTION SCAR TYPE UNKNOWN and High risk are both clinically associated with cesarean section, indicating that an unidentified type of cesarean scar and a high‑risk patient status are relevant factors in the consideration, planning, and postoperative management of cesarean deliveries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 213
retrieved_docs.content: complications involve serous choroidal detachment (انفصال المشيمية المصلية), general choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين), and choroidal tear. Language‑specific eponymous entities also include familial choroidal membrane disorder (الأَتْلامُ وِعائِيَّةُ الشَّكل بالمشيمية) and choroidal rupture (التمزق المشيمي). Collectively, these interrelated entities provide a comprehensive framework for diagnosing, classifying, and managing ocular, vascular, and systemic diseases that affect the choroid, retina, and related structures, emphasizing the choroidea as the common locus for inflammatory, degenerative, hemorrhagic, depositional, traumatic, postoperative, metastatic, and scar‑forming processes.
